Learning API

Training Library containing training routines.
xgboost.train(params, dtrain, num_boost_round=10, *, evals=None, obj=None, maximize=None, early_stopping_rounds=None, evals_result=None, verbose_eval=True, xgb_model=None, callbacks=None, custom_metric=None)
      Train a booster with given parameters.


          Parameters
               • params (Dict(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Dict)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)]) – Booster params.
                 • dtrain (DMatrix(#page=149)) – Data to be trained.
                 • num_boost_round (int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)) – Number of boosting iterations.
               • evals (Sequence(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Sequence)[Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[DMatrix(#page=149), str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)]] | None) – List of validation sets for which

                     metrics will evaluated during training. Validation metrics will help us track the performance
                    of the model.
               • obj  (Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)[[ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray), DMatrix(#page=149)], Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray), ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray)]] | None)  –

                     Custom objective function. See Custom Objective(#page=96) for details.
                • maximize (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool) | None) – Whether to maximize custom_metric.
                 • early_stopping_rounds (int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) | None) – Activates early stopping. Validation metric

                    needs to improve at least once in every early_stopping_rounds round(s) to continue training.
                     Requires at least one item in evals.
                     The method returns the model from the last iteration (not the best one). Use custom callback                   EarlyStopping(#page=248) or model slicing(#page=168) if the best model is desired. If there’s more than one 
                     item in evals, the last entry will be used for early stopping.
                    If there’s more than one metric in the eval_metric parameter given in params, the last metric                      will be used for early stopping.
                  If early stopping occurs, the model will have two additional fields: bst.best_score, bst.
                best_iteration.
               • evals_result     (Dict(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Dict)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), Dict(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Dict)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)] | List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)[Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float),
                 float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]]]] | None) – This dictionary stores the evaluation results of all the items in 
                      watchlist.
                 Example: with a watchlist containing [(dtest,'eval'), (dtrain,'train')] and a parameter containing ('eval_metric':'logloss'), the evals_result returns 

```
 {'train': {'logloss': ['0.48253', '0.35953']},
```

                • verbose_eval (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool) | int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) | None) – Requires at least one item in evals.

                     If verbose_eval is True then the evaluation metric on the validation set is printed at each
                     boosting stage.



--- PAGE 180 ---
                     If verbose_eval   is an integer then the evaluation metric on the validation set is printed at
                     every given verbose_eval  boosting stage. The last boosting stage / the boosting stage found
                     by using early_stopping_rounds    is also printed.
                   Example: with verbose_eval=4 and at least one item in evals, an evaluation metric is                      printed every 4 boosting stages, instead of every boosting stage.
               • xgb_model (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) | PathLike(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/os.html#os.PathLike) | Booster(#page=167) | bytearray(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#bytearray) | None) – Xgb model to be

                     loaded before training (allows training continuation).
                • callbacks (Sequence(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Sequence)[TrainingCallback(#page=246)] | None) – List of callback functions that

                     are applied at end of each iteration. It is possible to use predefined callbacks by using Call-(#page=246)
                  back API(#page=246).


```
 ò  Note
  States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback objects can
  not be reused for multiple training sessions without reinitialization or deepcopy.
 for params in parameters_grid:
     # be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
     callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
     xgboost.train(params, Xy, callbacks=callbacks)
```

               • custom_metric (Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)[[ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray), DMatrix(#page=149)], Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]] | None)
                    – Custom metric function. See Custom Metric(#page=96) for details. The metric receives transformed
                     prediction (after applying the reverse link function) when using a builtin objective, and raw                     output when using a custom objective.

          Returns

              Booster
          Return type

                a trained booster model
xgboost.cv(params, dtrain, num_boost_round=10, *, nfold=3, stratified=False, folds=None, metrics=(), obj=None, maximize=None, early_stopping_rounds=None, fpreproc=None, as_pandas=True, verbose_eval=None, show_stdv=True, seed=0, callbacks=None, shuffle=True, custom_metric=None) Cross-validation with given parameters.


          Parameters
                • params (dict(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#dict)) – Booster params.
                 • dtrain (DMatrix(#page=149)) – Data to be trained. Only the DMatrix(#page=149) without external memory is

                    supported.
                 • num_boost_round (int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)) – Number of boosting iterations.
                • nfold (int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)) – Number of folds in CV.
                 • stratified (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool)) – Perform stratified sampling.
               • folds (a KFold or StratifiedKFold instance or list(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#list) of fold indices) –

                     Sklearn KFolds or StratifiedKFolds object. Alternatively may explicitly pass sample indices
                     for each fold. For n folds, folds should be a length n list of tuples. Each tuple is (in,out)                     where in is a list of indices to be used as the training samples for the n th fold and out is a                       list of indices to be used as the testing samples for the n th fold.



--- PAGE 181 ---
               • metrics (string or list(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#list) of strings) – Evaluation metrics to be watched in CV.
               • obj  (Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)[[ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray), DMatrix(#page=149)], Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray), ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray)]] | None)  –

                     Custom objective function. See Custom Objective(#page=96) for details.
                 • maximize (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool)) – Whether to maximize the evaluataion metric (score or error).
                  • early_stopping_rounds (int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)) – Activates early stopping. Cross-Validation metric (av-
                    erage of validation metric computed over CV folds) needs to improve at least once in every                    early_stopping_rounds round(s) to continue training. The last entry in the evaluation history will represent the best iteration. If there’s more than one metric in the eval_metric                    parameter given in params, the last metric will be used for early stopping.
                 • fpreproc (function) – Preprocessing function that takes (dtrain, dtest, param) and returns

                     transformed versions of those.
                 • as_pandas (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool), default True) – Return pd.DataFrame when pandas is installed. If

                     False or pandas is not installed, return np.ndarray
               • verbose_eval (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool), int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int), or None, default None) – Whether to display the

                     progress. If None, progress will be displayed when np.ndarray is returned. If True, progress
                      will be displayed at boosting stage. If an integer is given, progress will be displayed at every                     given verbose_eval boosting stage.
                • show_stdv (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool), default True) – Whether to display the standard deviation in

                     progress. Results are not affected, and always contains std.
                • seed (int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)) – Seed used to generate the folds (passed to numpy.random.seed).
                • callbacks (Sequence(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Sequence)[TrainingCallback(#page=246)] | None) – List of callback functions that

                     are applied at end of each iteration. It is possible to use predefined callbacks by using Call-(#page=246)
                  back API(#page=246).


```
 ò  Note
  States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback objects can
  not be reused for multiple training sessions without reinitialization or deepcopy.
 for params in parameters_grid:
     # be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
     callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
     xgboost.train(params, Xy, callbacks=callbacks)
```

                 • shuffle (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool)) – Shuffle data before creating folds.
               • custom_metric (Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)[[ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray), DMatrix(#page=149)], Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]] | None)

```

                    A threshold for deciding whether XGBoost should use one-hot encoding based split for categorical data. When number of categories is lesser than the threshold then one-hot encod-                     ing is chosen, otherwise the categories will be partitioned into children nodes. Also, enable_categorical needs to be set to have categorical feature support. See Categorical Data(#page=50)                     and Parameters for Categorical Feature(#page=129) for details.
               • max_cat_threshold (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Added in version 1.7.0.

                    The strategy used for training multi-target models, including multi-target regression and                      multi-class classification. See Multiple Outputs(#page=54) for more information.

                 – one_output_per_tree: One model for each target.
                  – multi_output_tree: Use multi-target trees.
                  • eval_metric                (Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union) [ str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) , List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)  [Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union)  [str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) ,  Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)  ]],  Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)   ,
                     NoneType]  ) – Added in version 1.6.0.

                      Metric used for monitoring the training result and early stopping. It can be a string or list of
                    strings as names of predefined metric in XGBoost (See XGBoost Parameters(#page=123)), one of the metrics in sklearn.metrics(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/api/sklearn.metrics.html#module-sklearn.metrics), or any other user defined metric that looks like sklearn.metrics.
                     If custom objective is also provided, then custom metric should implement the corresponding                      reverse link function.
                    Unlike the scoring parameter commonly used in scikit-learn, when a callable object is provided, it’s assumed to be a cost function and by default XGBoost will minimize the result                      during early stopping.
                    For advanced usage on Early stopping like directly choosing to maximize instead of minimize, see xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping(#page=248).



--- PAGE 185 ---

                  See Custom Objective and Evaluation Metric(#page=96) and Custom objective and metric(#page=30) for more
                    information.


```
 from sklearn.datasets import load_diabetes
 from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
 X, y = load_diabetes(return_X_y=True)
 reg = xgb.XGBRegressor(
     tree_method="hist",
     eval_metric=mean_absolute_error,
 )
 reg.fit(X, y, eval_set=[(X, y)])
```

               • early_stopping_rounds (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Added in version 1.6.0.

                    – Activates early stopping.  Validation metric needs to improve at least once in every
                       early_stopping_rounds    round(s) to continue training.  Requires at least one item in
                       eval_set in fit()(#page=187) .
                  – If early stopping occurs, the model will have two additional attributes: best_score(#page=186) and
                    best_iteration(#page=186). These are used by the predict()(#page=189) and apply()(#page=186) methods to determine the optimal number of trees during inference. If users want to access the full model 
                        (including trees built after early stopping), they can specify the iteration_range in these
                        inference methods. In addition, other utilities like model plotting can also use the entire                     model.

                    – If you prefer to discard the trees after best_iteration, consider using the callback function
                  xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping(#page=248).

                    – If there’s more than one item in eval_set, the last entry will be used for early stopping. If

                       there’s more than one metric in eval_metric, the last metric will be used for early stopping.
               • callbacks (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)[xgboost.callback.TrainingCallback(#page=246)]]) – List of

                     callback functions that are applied at end of each iteration. It is possible to use predefined
                    callbacks by using Callback API(#page=246).


```
 ò  Note
  States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback objects can
  not be reused for multiple training sessions without reinitialization or deepcopy.
 for params in parameters_grid:
     # be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
     callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
     reg = xgboost.XGBRegressor(**params, callbacks=callbacks)
     reg.fit(X, y)
```

                • kwargs (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)]) – Keyword arguments for XGBoost Booster object. Full docu-

                     mentation of parameters can be found here(#page=123). Attempting to set a parameter via the constructor
                     args and **kwargs dict simultaneously will result in a TypeError.

 ò  Note
  **kwargs unsupported by scikit-learn
```


--- PAGE 186 ---

```
  **kwargs is unsupported by scikit-learn. We do not guarantee that parameters passed
  via this argument will interact properly with scikit-learn.
 ò  Note
  Custom objective function
  A custom objective function can be provided for the  objective   parameter. In this
  case, it should have the signature objective(y_true, y_pred) -> [grad, hess]
  or objective(y_true, y_pred, *, sample_weight) -> [grad, hess]               :
  y_true: array_like of shape [n_samples]
        The target values
  y_pred: array_like of shape [n_samples]
        The predicted values
  sample_weight :
        Optional sample weights.
  grad: array_like of shape [n_samples]
        The value of the gradient for each sample point.
  hess: array_like of shape [n_samples]
        The value of the second derivative for each sample point
  Note that, if the custom objective produces negative values for the Hessian, these will
  be clipped. If the objective is non-convex, one might also consider using the expected
  Hessian (Fisher information) instead.
```

     apply(X, iteration_range=None) 
           Return the predicted leaf every tree for each sample. If the model is trained with early stopping, then
          best_iteration(#page=186) is used automatically.


             Parameters
                   • X (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Input features matrix. See Markers(#page=285) for a list of supported types.
                     • iteration_range       (Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)  [int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)  |  integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer)  ,  int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)  | integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer)   ] | None   )  –   See
                  predict()(#page=189).

             Returns

                    X_leaves – For each datapoint x in X and for each tree, return the index of the leaf x ends up
                   in. Leaves are numbered within [0; 2**(self.max_depth+1)), possibly with gaps in the                   numbering.

              Return type

                    array_like, shape=[n_samples, n_trees]
     property best_iteration:  int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) 
            The best iteration obtained by early stopping. This attribute is 0-based, for instance if the best iteration is
             the first round, then best_iteration is 0.
     property best_score:  float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float) 
            The best score obtained by early stopping.
     property coef_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray) 
            Coefficients property



--- PAGE 187 ---

```
 ò  Note
  Coefficients are defined only for linear learners
  Coefficients are only defined when the linear model is chosen as base learner (booster=gblinear ). It is
  not defined for other base learner types, such as tree learners (booster=gbtree  ).

             Returns

                   coef_
              Return type

     evals_result()

            Return the evaluation results.

            If eval_set is passed to the fit()(#page=187) function, you can call evals_result() to get evaluation results for all            passed eval_sets. When eval_metric is also passed to the fit()(#page=187) function, the evals_result will contain            the eval_metrics passed to the fit()(#page=187) function.
            The returned evaluation result is a dictionary:


```
 {'validation_0': {'logloss': ['0.604835', '0.531479']},
  'validation_1': {'logloss': ['0.41965', '0.17686']}}
```


              Return type

                     evals_result

     property feature_importances_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray) 
           Feature importances property, return depends on importance_type parameter. When model trained with
            multi-class/multi-label/multi-target dataset, the feature importance is “averaged” over all targets. The “average” is defined based on the importance type. For instance, if the importance type is “total_gain”, then             the score is sum of loss change for each split from all trees.

             Returns
                    • feature_importances_ (array of shape [n_features] except for multi-class)
                     • linear model, which returns an array with shape (n_features, n_classes)
     property feature_names_in_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray) 
           Names of features seen during fit()(#page=187). Defined only when X has feature names that are all strings.
fit(X, y, *, sample_weight=None, base_margin=None, eval_set=None, verbose=True, xgb_model=None, sample_weight_eval_set=None, base_margin_eval_set=None, feature_weights=None)
            Fit gradient boosting model.
            Note that calling fit()  multiple times will cause the model object to be re-fit from scratch. To resume
            training from a previous checkpoint, explicitly pass xgb_model   argument.

             Parameters
                   • X (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Input feature matrix. See Markers(#page=285) for a list of supported types.

                      When the tree_method is set to hist, internally, the QuantileDMatrix(#page=155) will be used in-
                      stead of the DMatrix(#page=149) for conserving memory. However, this has performance implications                         when the device of input data is not matched with algorithm. For instance, if the input is a 


--- PAGE 188 ---

                     numpy array on CPU but cuda is used for training, then the data is first processed on CPU
                       then transferred to GPU.
                   • y (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Labels
                  • sample_weight (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any) | None) – instance weights
                   • base_margin (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any) | None) – Global bias for each instance. See Intercept(#page=114) for details.
                 • eval_set (Sequence(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Sequence)[Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any), Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)]] | None) – A list of (X, y) tuple pairs to use

                        as validation sets, for which metrics will be computed. Validation metrics will help us track
                      the performance of the model.
                   • verbose (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool) | int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) | None) – If verbose is True and an evaluation set is used, the

                        evaluation metric measured on the validation set is printed to stdout at each boosting stage.
                       If verbose is an integer, the evaluation metric is printed at each verbose boosting stage.
                       The last boosting stage / the boosting stage found by using early_stopping_rounds is also                         printed.
                  • xgb_model (Booster(#page=167) | XGBModel | str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) | None) – file name of stored XGBoost

                       model or ‘Booster’ instance XGBoost model to be loaded before training (allows training
                       continuation).
                  • sample_weight_eval_set (Sequence(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Sequence)[Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)] | None) – A list of the form [L_1, L_2,

```
..., L_n], where each L_i is an array like object storing instance weights for the i-th validation set.
• base_margin_eval_set      (Sequence(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Sequence)  [ Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any) ] | None  ) – A list of the form [M_1, M_2,
..., M_n], where each M_i is an array like object storing base margin for the i-th validation
set.
```

                  • feature_weights (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any) | None) – Deprecated since version 3.0.0.

                      Use feature_weights in __init__() or set_params()(#page=192) instead.

              Return type
               XGBModel

     load_model(fname) 
            Load the model from a file or a bytearray.
           The model is saved in an XGBoost internal format which is universal among the various XGBoost interfaces. Auxiliary attributes of the Python Booster object (such as feature_names) are only saved when            using JSON or UBJSON (default) format. Also, parameters that are not part of the model (like metrics,            max_depth, etc) are not saved, see Model IO(#page=28) for more info.


```
 model.save_model("model.json")
 model.load_model("model.json")
 # or
 model.save_model("model.ubj")
 model.load_model("model.ubj")
 # or
 buf = model.save_raw()
 model.load_model(buf)
```


             Parameters

fname (PathLike(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/os.html#os.PathLike) | bytearray(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#bytearray) | str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)) – Input file name or memory buffer(see also save_raw)

              Return type
               None

     property n_features_in_:  int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) 
          Number of features seen during fit()(#page=187).

     predict(X, *, output_margin=False, validate_features=True, base_margin=None, iteration_range=None)
            Predict with X. If the model is trained with early stopping, then best_iteration(#page=186)      is used automatically.
            The estimator uses inplace_predict by default and falls back to using DMatrix(#page=149)   if devices between the data
            and the estimator don’t match.


```
 ò  Note
  This function is only thread safe for gbtree and dart.
```

             Parameters
                   • X (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Data to predict with. See Markers(#page=285) for a list of supported types.
                   • output_margin (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool)) – Whether to output the raw untransformed margin value.
                    • validate_features (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool)) – When this is True, validate that the Booster’s and data’s

                       feature_names are identical. Otherwise, it is assumed that the feature_names are the same.
                   • base_margin (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any) | None) – Global bias for each instance. See Intercept(#page=114) for details.
                 • iteration_range (Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) | integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer), int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) | integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer)] | None) – Specifies

                        which layer of trees are used in prediction. For example, if a random forest is trained with
                     100 rounds. Specifying iteration_range=(10, 20), then only the forests built during 
                        [10, 20) (half open set) rounds are used in this prediction.
                       Added in version 1.4.0.

              Return type

                    prediction

     save_model(fname)

 model.save_model("model.json")
 # or
 model.save_model("model.ubj")
```

              Return type

     score(X, y, sample_weight=None) 
            Return coefficient of determination(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/model_evaluation.html#r2-score) on test data.
          The coefficient of determination, 𝑅2, is defined as (1 − 𝑢𝑣 ), where 𝑢 is the residual sum of squares           ((y_true - y_pred)** 2).sum() and 𝑣 is the total sum of squares ((y_true - y_true.mean())           ** 2).sum(). The best possible score is 1.0 and it can be negative (because the model can be arbitrarily
            worse). A constant model that always predicts the expected value of y, disregarding the input features,            would get a 𝑅2 score of 0.0.



--- PAGE 191 ---

             Parameters
                     • X (array-like of shape (n_samples, n_features)              ) – Test samples. For some estimators this may be a precomputed kernel matrix or a list of generic objects instead with
                       shape  (n_samples, n_samples_fitted)        , where n_samples_fitted      is the number of
                       samples used in the fitting for the estimator.
                   • y (array-like of shape (n_samples,) or (n_samples, n_outputs)) – True

                       values for X.
                   • sample_weight (array-like of shape (n_samples,), default=None) – Sam-

                       ple weights.

             Returns

                    score – 𝑅2 of self.predict(X) w.r.t. y.

              Return type

                       float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)

        Notes
          The 𝑅2 score used when calling score on a regressor uses multioutput='uniform_average' from            version 0.23 to keep consistent with default value of r2_score()(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.r2_score.html#sklearn.metrics.r2_score). This influences the score method of           all the multioutput regressors (except for MultiOutputRegressor(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.multioutput.MultiOutputRegressor.html#sklearn.multioutput.MultiOutputRegressor)).
set_fit_request(*, base_margin='$UNCHANGED$', base_margin_eval_set='$UNCHANGED$', eval_set='$UNCHANGED$', feature_weights='$UNCHANGED$', sample_weight='$UNCHANGED$', sample_weight_eval_set='$UNCHANGED$', verbose='$UNCHANGED$', xgb_model='$UNCHANGED$') Configure whether metadata should be requested to be passed to the fit method.
            Note that this method is only relevant when this estimator is used as a sub-estimator within a meta-estimator(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/glossary.html#term-meta-estimator)            and metadata routing is enabled with enable_metadata_routing=True (see sklearn.set_config()(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.set_config.html#sklearn.set_config)).
           Please check the User Guide(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/metadata_routing.html#metadata-routing) on how the routing mechanism works.
            The options for each parameter are:
              • True: metadata is requested, and passed to fit if provided. The request is ignored if metadata is not
                provided.
             • False: metadata is not requested and the meta-estimator will not pass it to fit.
              • None: metadata is not requested, and the meta-estimator will raise an error if the user provides it.
             • str: metadata should be passed to the meta-estimator with this given alias instead of the original
              name.
           The default (sklearn.utils.metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) retains the existing request. This allows             you to change the request for some parameters and not others.
            Added in version 1.3.

             Parameters
                   • base_margin         (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.
                   metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) – Metadata routing for base_margin parameter 
                      in fit.
                   • base_margin_eval_set     (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.
                     utils.metadata_routing.UNCHANGED)      –      Metadata      routing      for                     base_margin_eval_set parameter in fit.



--- PAGE 192 ---
                   • eval_set            (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.
                   metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) – Metadata routing for eval_set parameter in                   fit.
                   • feature_weights    (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.
                    metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) – Metadata routing for feature_weights parameter                       in fit.
                   • sample_weight      (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.
                    metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) – Metadata routing for sample_weight parameter in                   fit.
                   • sample_weight_eval_set   (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.
                     utils.metadata_routing.UNCHANGED)      –      Metadata      routing      for                     sample_weight_eval_set parameter in fit.
                   • verbose             (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.

class xgboost.XGBClassifier(*, objective='binary:logistic', **kwargs) 
     Bases: ClassifierMixin(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.base.ClassifierMixin.html#sklearn.base.ClassifierMixin), XGBModel
      Implementation of the scikit-learn API for XGBoost classification. See  Using the Scikit-Learn Estimator Inter-(#page=143)
      face(#page=143) for more information.


          Parameters
                • n_estimators (Optional[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Number of boosting rounds.
               • max_depth (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Maximum tree depth for base learners.
               • max_leaves (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Maximum number of leaves; 0 indicates no limit.
                • max_bin (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – If using histogram-based algorithm, maximum number of

                     bins per feature
               • grow_policy (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)]) – Tree growing policy.

                   – depthwise: Favors splitting at nodes closest to the node,
                  – lossguide: Favors splitting at nodes with highest loss change.
               • learning_rate (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – Boosting learning rate (xgb’s “eta”)
                • verbosity (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – The degree of verbosity. Valid values are 0 (silent) - 3

                    (debug).
               • objective   (Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), xgboost.sklearn._SklObjWProto, Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)[[Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any),
                Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)], Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[numpy.ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray), numpy.ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray)]], NoneType]) – Specify the learning task and the corresponding learning objective or a custom objective function to be 
                    used.
                   For custom objective, see Custom Objective and Evaluation Metric(#page=96) and Custom objective(#page=30)                     and metric(#page=30) for more information, along with the end note for function signatures.
                • booster (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)]) – Specify which booster to use: gbtree, gblinear or dart.
                • tree_method (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)]) – Specify which tree method to use. Default to auto. If

                     this parameter is set to default, XGBoost will choose the most conservative option available.
                    It’s recommended to study this option from the parameters document tree method(#page=138)                 • n_jobs (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Number of parallel threads used to run xgboost. When used

                   with other Scikit-Learn algorithms like grid search, you may choose which algorithm to
                     parallelize and balance the threads. Creating thread contention will significantly slow down                     both algorithms.
                • gamma (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – (min_split_loss) Minimum loss reduction required to make

                      a further partition on a leaf node of the tree.
                • min_child_weight (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – Minimum sum of instance weight(hessian)

                     needed in a child.
               • max_delta_step (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – Maximum delta step we allow each tree’s weight

                     estimation to be.
               • subsample (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – Subsample ratio of the training instance.
               • sampling_method (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)]) – Sampling method. Used only by the GPU version

                   of hist tree method.

                 – uniform: Select random training instances uniformly.



--- PAGE 195 ---
                 – gradient_based: Select random training instances with higher probability

                          when the gradient and hessian are larger. (cf. CatBoost)
               • colsample_bytree (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – Subsample ratio of columns when constructing

                      each tree.
               • colsample_bylevel (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – Subsample ratio of columns for each level.
               • colsample_bynode (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – Subsample ratio of columns for each split.
               • reg_alpha (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – L1 regularization term on weights (xgb’s alpha).
               • reg_lambda (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – L2 regularization term on weights (xgb’s lambda).
               • scale_pos_weight (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)]) – Balancing of positive and negative weights.
               • base_score (Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float), List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)[float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)], NoneType]) – The initial prediction score

                      of all instances, global bias.
               • random_state     (Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union)[numpy.random.mtrand.RandomState(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/legacy.html#numpy.random.RandomState), numpy.random.(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/generator.html#numpy.random.Generator)
                _generator.Generator(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/generator.html#numpy.random.Generator), int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int), NoneType]) – Random number seed.

  Using gblinear booster with shotgun updater is nondeterministic as it uses Hogwild algorithm.

                 • feature_weights (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Weight for each feature, defines the proba-

                     bility of each feature being selected when colsample is being used. All values must be greater

                • max_cat_to_onehot (Optional[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Added in version 1.6.0.


```
 ò  Note
  This parameter is experimental
```

                    A threshold for deciding whether XGBoost should use one-hot encoding based split for categorical data. When number of categories is lesser than the threshold then one-hot encod-                     ing is chosen, otherwise the categories will be partitioned into children nodes. Also, enable_categorical needs to be set to have categorical feature support. See Categorical Data(#page=50)                     and Parameters for Categorical Feature(#page=129) for details.
               • max_cat_threshold (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Added in version 1.7.0.


```
 ò  Note
  This parameter is experimental
```

                   Maximum number of categories considered for each split. Used only by partition-based                      splits for preventing over-fitting. Also, enable_categorical needs to be set to have categorical                      feature support. See Categorical Data(#page=50) and Parameters for Categorical Feature(#page=129) for details.
               • multi_strategy (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)]) – Added in version 2.0.0.


```
 ò  Note
  This parameter is working-in-progress.
```

                    The strategy used for training multi-target models, including multi-target regression and                      multi-class classification. See Multiple Outputs(#page=54) for more information.

                 – one_output_per_tree: One model for each target.
                  – multi_output_tree: Use multi-target trees.
                  • eval_metric                (Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union) [ str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) , List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)  [Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union)  [str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) ,  Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)  ]],  Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)   ,
                     NoneType]  ) – Added in version 1.6.0.

                      Metric used for monitoring the training result and early stopping. It can be a string or list of
                    strings as names of predefined metric in XGBoost (See XGBoost Parameters(#page=123)), one of the metrics in sklearn.metrics(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/api/sklearn.metrics.html#module-sklearn.metrics), or any other user defined metric that looks like sklearn.metrics.
                     If custom objective is also provided, then custom metric should implement the corresponding                      reverse link function.
                    Unlike the scoring parameter commonly used in scikit-learn, when a callable object is provided, it’s assumed to be a cost function and by default XGBoost will minimize the result                      during early stopping.
                    For advanced usage on Early stopping like directly choosing to maximize instead of minimize, see xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping(#page=248).



--- PAGE 197 ---

                  See Custom Objective and Evaluation Metric(#page=96) and Custom objective and metric(#page=30) for more
                    information.


```
 from sklearn.datasets import load_diabetes
 from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
 X, y = load_diabetes(return_X_y=True)
 reg = xgb.XGBRegressor(
     tree_method="hist",
     eval_metric=mean_absolute_error,
 )
 reg.fit(X, y, eval_set=[(X, y)])
```

               • early_stopping_rounds (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Added in version 1.6.0.

                    – Activates early stopping.  Validation metric needs to improve at least once in every
                       early_stopping_rounds    round(s) to continue training.  Requires at least one item in
                       eval_set in fit()(#page=199) .
                  – If early stopping occurs, the model will have two additional attributes: best_score(#page=198) and
                    best_iteration(#page=198). These are used by the predict()(#page=201) and apply()(#page=198) methods to determine the optimal number of trees during inference. If users want to access the full model 
                        (including trees built after early stopping), they can specify the iteration_range in these
                        inference methods. In addition, other utilities like model plotting can also use the entire                     model.

                    – If you prefer to discard the trees after best_iteration, consider using the callback function
                  xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping(#page=248).

                    – If there’s more than one item in eval_set, the last entry will be used for early stopping. If

                       there’s more than one metric in eval_metric, the last metric will be used for early stopping.
               • callbacks (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)[xgboost.callback.TrainingCallback(#page=246)]]) – List of

                     callback functions that are applied at end of each iteration. It is possible to use predefined
                    callbacks by using Callback API(#page=246).


```
 ò  Note
  States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback objects can
  not be reused for multiple training sessions without reinitialization or deepcopy.
 for params in parameters_grid:
     # be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
     callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
     reg = xgboost.XGBRegressor(**params, callbacks=callbacks)
     reg.fit(X, y)
```

                • kwargs (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)]) – Keyword arguments for XGBoost Booster object. Full docu-

                     mentation of parameters can be found here(#page=123). Attempting to set a parameter via the constructor
                     args and **kwargs dict simultaneously will result in a TypeError.


```
 ò  Note
  **kwargs unsupported by scikit-learn
```


--- PAGE 198 ---

```
  **kwargs is unsupported by scikit-learn. We do not guarantee that parameters passed
  via this argument will interact properly with scikit-learn.
 ò  Note
  Custom objective function
  A custom objective function can be provided for the  objective   parameter. In this
  case, it should have the signature objective(y_true, y_pred) -> [grad, hess]
  or objective(y_true, y_pred, *, sample_weight) -> [grad, hess]               :
  y_true: array_like of shape [n_samples]
        The target values
  y_pred: array_like of shape [n_samples]
        The predicted values
  sample_weight :
        Optional sample weights.
  grad: array_like of shape [n_samples]
        The value of the gradient for each sample point.
  hess: array_like of shape [n_samples]
        The value of the second derivative for each sample point
  Note that, if the custom objective produces negative values for the Hessian, these will
  be clipped. If the objective is non-convex, one might also consider using the expected
  Hessian (Fisher information) instead.
```

     apply(X, iteration_range=None) 
           Return the predicted leaf every tree for each sample. If the model is trained with early stopping, then
          best_iteration(#page=198) is used automatically.


             Parameters
                   • X (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Input features matrix. See Markers(#page=285) for a list of supported types.
                     • iteration_range       (Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)  [int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)  |  integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer)  ,  int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)  | integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer)   ] | None   )  –   See
                  predict()(#page=201).

             Returns

                    X_leaves – For each datapoint x in X and for each tree, return the index of the leaf x ends up
                   in. Leaves are numbered within [0; 2**(self.max_depth+1)), possibly with gaps in the                   numbering.

              Return type

                    array_like, shape=[n_samples, n_trees]
     property best_iteration:  int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) 
            The best iteration obtained by early stopping. This attribute is 0-based, for instance if the best iteration is
             the first round, then best_iteration is 0.
     property best_score:  float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float) 
            The best score obtained by early stopping.
     property coef_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray) 
            Coefficients property



--- PAGE 199 ---

```
 ò  Note
  Coefficients are defined only for linear learners
  Coefficients are only defined when the linear model is chosen as base learner (booster=gblinear ). It is
  not defined for other base learner types, such as tree learners (booster=gbtree  ).
```

             Returns

                   coef_
              Return type

                   array of shape [n_features] or [n_classes, n_features]

     evals_result()

            Return the evaluation results.

            If eval_set is passed to the fit()(#page=199) function, you can call evals_result() to get evaluation results for all            passed eval_sets. When eval_metric is also passed to the fit()(#page=199) function, the evals_result will contain            the eval_metrics passed to the fit()(#page=199) function.
            The returned evaluation result is a dictionary:


```
 {'validation_0': {'logloss': ['0.604835', '0.531479']},
  'validation_1': {'logloss': ['0.41965', '0.17686']}}
```


              Return type

                     evals_result

     property feature_importances_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray) 
           Feature importances property, return depends on importance_type parameter. When model trained with
            multi-class/multi-label/multi-target dataset, the feature importance is “averaged” over all targets. The “average” is defined based on the importance type. For instance, if the importance type is “total_gain”, then             the score is sum of loss change for each split from all trees.

             Returns
                    • feature_importances_ (array of shape [n_features] except for multi-class)
                     • linear model, which returns an array with shape (n_features, n_classes)
     property feature_names_in_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray) 
           Names of features seen during fit()(#page=199). Defined only when X has feature names that are all strings.
fit(X, y, *, sample_weight=None, base_margin=None, eval_set=None, verbose=True, xgb_model=None, sample_weight_eval_set=None, base_margin_eval_set=None, feature_weights=None)

             Fit gradient boosting classifier.
            Note that calling fit()  multiple times will cause the model object to be re-fit from scratch. To resume
            training from a previous checkpoint, explicitly pass xgb_model   argument.

             Parameters
                   • X (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Input feature matrix. See Markers(#page=285) for a list of supported types.

                      When the tree_method is set to hist, internally, the QuantileDMatrix(#page=155) will be used in-
                      stead of the DMatrix(#page=149) for conserving memory. However, this has performance implications                         when the device of input data is not matched with algorithm. For instance, if the input is a 


--- PAGE 200 ---

                     numpy array on CPU but cuda is used for training, then the data is first processed on CPU
                       then transferred to GPU.
                   • y (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Labels
                  • sample_weight (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any) | None) – instance weights
                   • base_margin (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any) | None) – Global bias for each instance. See Intercept(#page=114) for details.
                 • eval_set (Sequence(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Sequence)[Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any), Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)]] | None) – A list of (X, y) tuple pairs to use

                        as validation sets, for which metrics will be computed. Validation metrics will help us track
                      the performance of the model.
                   • verbose (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool) | int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) | None) – If verbose is True and an evaluation set is used, the

                        evaluation metric measured on the validation set is printed to stdout at each boosting stage.
                       If verbose is an integer, the evaluation metric is printed at each verbose boosting stage.
                       The last boosting stage / the boosting stage found by using early_stopping_rounds is also                         printed.
                  • xgb_model (Booster(#page=167) | str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) | XGBModel | None) – file name of stored XGBoost

                       continuation).

```
..., L_n], where each L_i is an array like object storing instance weights for the i-th validation set.

     load_model(fname) 
            Load the model from a file or a bytearray.
           The model is saved in an XGBoost internal format which is universal among the various XGBoost interfaces. Auxiliary attributes of the Python Booster object (such as feature_names) are only saved when            using JSON or UBJSON (default) format. Also, parameters that are not part of the model (like metrics,            max_depth, etc) are not saved, see Model IO(#page=28) for more info.


```
 model.save_model("model.json")
 model.load_model("model.json")
 # or
 model.save_model("model.ubj")
 model.load_model("model.ubj")
 # or
 buf = model.save_raw()
 model.load_model(buf)
```


             Parameters

fname (PathLike(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/os.html#os.PathLike) | bytearray(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#bytearray) | str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)) – Input file name or memory buffer(see also save_raw)

              Return type
               None

     predict(X, *, output_margin=False, validate_features=True, base_margin=None, iteration_range=None)
            Predict with X. If the model is trained with early stopping, then best_iteration(#page=198)      is used automatically.
            The estimator uses inplace_predict by default and falls back to using DMatrix(#page=149)   if devices between the data
            and the estimator don’t match.


```
 ò  Note
  This function is only thread safe for gbtree and dart.
```

             Parameters
                   • X (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Data to predict with. See Markers(#page=285) for a list of supported types.
                   • output_margin (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool)) – Whether to output the raw untransformed margin value.
                    • validate_features (bool(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#bool)) – When this is True, validate that the Booster’s and data’s

                       feature_names are identical. Otherwise, it is assumed that the feature_names are the same.
                   • base_margin (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any) | None) – Global bias for each instance. See Intercept(#page=114) for details.
                 • iteration_range (Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) | integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer), int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) | integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer)] | None) – Specifies

                        which layer of trees are used in prediction. For example, if a random forest is trained with
                     100 rounds. Specifying iteration_range=(10, 20), then only the forests built during 
                        [10, 20) (half open set) rounds are used in this prediction.
                       Added in version 1.4.0.

              Return type

                    prediction

     score(X, y, sample_weight=None) 
           Return accuracy(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/model_evaluation.html#accuracy-score) on provided data and labels.
            In multi-label classification, this is the subset accuracy which is a harsh metric since you require for each             sample that each label set be correctly predicted.

             Parameters
                   • X (array-like of shape (n_samples, n_features)) – Test samples.
                   • y (array-like of shape (n_samples,) or (n_samples, n_outputs)) – True

                       labels for X.
                   • sample_weight (array-like of shape (n_samples,), default=None) – Sam-

                       ple weights.

             Returns

                    score – Mean accuracy of self.predict(X) w.r.t. y.

              Return type

                       float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float)
set_fit_request(*, base_margin='$UNCHANGED$', base_margin_eval_set='$UNCHANGED$', eval_set='$UNCHANGED$', feature_weights='$UNCHANGED$', sample_weight='$UNCHANGED$', sample_weight_eval_set='$UNCHANGED$', verbose='$UNCHANGED$', xgb_model='$UNCHANGED$') Configure whether metadata should be requested to be passed to the fit method.
            Note that this method is only relevant when this estimator is used as a sub-estimator within a meta-estimator(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/glossary.html#term-meta-estimator)            and metadata routing is enabled with enable_metadata_routing=True (see sklearn.set_config()(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.set_config.html#sklearn.set_config)).
           Please check the User Guide(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/metadata_routing.html#metadata-routing) on how the routing mechanism works.
            The options for each parameter are:
              • True: metadata is requested, and passed to fit if provided. The request is ignored if metadata is not
                provided.
             • False: metadata is not requested and the meta-estimator will not pass it to fit.



--- PAGE 204 ---
              • None: metadata is not requested, and the meta-estimator will raise an error if the user provides it.
             • str: metadata should be passed to the meta-estimator with this given alias instead of the original
              name.
           The default (sklearn.utils.metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) retains the existing request. This allows             you to change the request for some parameters and not others.
            Added in version 1.3.

             Parameters
                   • base_margin         (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.
                   metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) – Metadata routing for base_margin parameter 
                      in fit.
                   • base_margin_eval_set     (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.
                     utils.metadata_routing.UNCHANGED)      –      Metadata      routing      for                     base_margin_eval_set parameter in fit.
                   • eval_set            (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.
                   metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) – Metadata routing for eval_set parameter in                   fit.
                   • feature_weights    (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.
                    metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) – Metadata routing for feature_weights parameter                       in fit.
                   • sample_weight      (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.utils.
                    metadata_routing.UNCHANGED) – Metadata routing for sample_weight parameter in                   fit.
                   • sample_weight_eval_set   (str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str), True, False, or None, default=sklearn.
                     utils.metadata_routing.UNCHANGED)      –      Metadata      routing      for                     sample_weight_eval_set parameter in fit.

                    A threshold for deciding whether XGBoost should use one-hot encoding based split for categorical data. When number of categories is lesser than the threshold then one-hot encod-                     ing is chosen, otherwise the categories will be partitioned into children nodes. Also, enable_categorical needs to be set to have categorical feature support. See Categorical Data(#page=50)                     and Parameters for Categorical Feature(#page=129) for details.
               • max_cat_threshold (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Added in version 1.7.0.

 ò  Note
  This parameter is experimental
```

                   Maximum number of categories considered for each split. Used only by partition-based                      splits for preventing over-fitting. Also, enable_categorical needs to be set to have categorical                      feature support. See Categorical Data(#page=50) and Parameters for Categorical Feature(#page=129) for details.
               • multi_strategy (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str)]) – Added in version 2.0.0.

                    The strategy used for training multi-target models, including multi-target regression and                      multi-class classification. See Multiple Outputs(#page=54) for more information.

                 – one_output_per_tree: One model for each target.
                  – multi_output_tree: Use multi-target trees.
                  • eval_metric                (Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union) [ str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) , List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)  [Union(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Union)  [str(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/stdtypes.html#str) ,  Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)  ]],  Callable(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Callable)   ,
                     NoneType]  ) – Added in version 1.6.0.

                      Metric used for monitoring the training result and early stopping. It can be a string or list of
                    strings as names of predefined metric in XGBoost (See XGBoost Parameters(#page=123)), one of the metrics in sklearn.metrics(https://scikit-learn.org/stable/api/sklearn.metrics.html#module-sklearn.metrics), or any other user defined metric that looks like sklearn.metrics.
                     If custom objective is also provided, then custom metric should implement the corresponding                      reverse link function.
                    Unlike the scoring parameter commonly used in scikit-learn, when a callable object is provided, it’s assumed to be a cost function and by default XGBoost will minimize the result 


--- PAGE 210 ---

                     during early stopping.
                    For advanced usage on Early stopping like directly choosing to maximize instead of minimize, see xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping(#page=248).
                  See Custom Objective and Evaluation Metric(#page=96) and Custom objective and metric(#page=30) for more                     information.


```
 from sklearn.datasets import load_diabetes
 from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
 X, y = load_diabetes(return_X_y=True)
 reg = xgb.XGBRegressor(
     tree_method="hist",
     eval_metric=mean_absolute_error,
 )
 reg.fit(X, y, eval_set=[(X, y)])
```

               • early_stopping_rounds (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)]) – Added in version 1.6.0.

                    – Activates early stopping.  Validation metric needs to improve at least once in every
                       early_stopping_rounds    round(s) to continue training.  Requires at least one item in
                       eval_set in fit()(#page=212) .
                  – If early stopping occurs, the model will have two additional attributes: best_score(#page=212) and
                    best_iteration(#page=212). These are used by the predict()(#page=216) and apply()(#page=211) methods to determine the optimal number of trees during inference. If users want to access the full model 
                        (including trees built after early stopping), they can specify the iteration_range in these
                        inference methods. In addition, other utilities like model plotting can also use the entire                     model.

                    – If you prefer to discard the trees after best_iteration, consider using the callback function
                  xgboost.callback.EarlyStopping(#page=248).

                    – If there’s more than one item in eval_set, the last entry will be used for early stopping. If

                       there’s more than one metric in eval_metric, the last metric will be used for early stopping.
               • callbacks (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[List(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.List)[xgboost.callback.TrainingCallback(#page=246)]]) – List of

                     callback functions that are applied at end of each iteration. It is possible to use predefined
                    callbacks by using Callback API(#page=246).


```
 ò  Note
  States in callback are not preserved during training, which means callback objects can
  not be reused for multiple training sessions without reinitialization or deepcopy.
 for params in parameters_grid:
     # be sure to (re)initialize the callbacks before each run
     callbacks = [xgb.callback.LearningRateScheduler(custom_rates)]
     reg = xgboost.XGBRegressor(**params, callbacks=callbacks)
     reg.fit(X, y)
```

                • kwargs (Optional(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Optional)[Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)]) – Keyword arguments for XGBoost Booster object. Full docu-

                     mentation of parameters can be found here(#page=123). Attempting to set a parameter via the constructor
                     args and **kwargs dict simultaneously will result in a TypeError.



--- PAGE 211 ---

```
 ò  Note
  **kwargs unsupported by scikit-learn
  **kwargs is unsupported by scikit-learn. We do not guarantee that parameters passed
  via this argument will interact properly with scikit-learn.
 ò  Note
  A custom objective function is currently not supported by XGBRanker.
 ò  Note
  Query group information is only required for ranking training but not prediction. Multiple groups can be predicted on a single call to predict()(#page=216)   .
```

                    When fitting the model with the  group  parameter, your data need to be sorted by the query
                     group first. group is an array that contains the size of each query group.
                     Similarly, when fitting the model with the qid parameter, the data should be sorted according
                     to query index and qid is an array that contains the query index for each training sample.
                     For example, if your original data look like:


                                        qid   label   features
                                        1    0     x_1
                                        1    1     x_2
                                        1    0     x_3
                                        2    0     x_4
                                        2    1     x_5
                                        2    1     x_6
                                        2    1     x_7

                   then fit()(#page=212) method can be called with either group array as [3, 4] or with qid as [1, 1,                    1, 2, 2, 2, 2], that is the qid column. Also, the qid can be a special column of input X                     instead of a separated parameter, see fit()(#page=212) for more info.
     apply(X, iteration_range=None) 
           Return the predicted leaf every tree for each sample. If the model is trained with early stopping, then
          best_iteration(#page=212) is used automatically.


             Parameters
                   • X (Any(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Any)) – Input features matrix. See Markers(#page=285) for a list of supported types.
                     • iteration_range       (Tuple(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/typing.html#typing.Tuple)  [int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)  |  integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer)  ,  int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int)  | integer(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.integer)   ] | None   )  –   See
                  predict()(#page=216).

             Returns

                    X_leaves – For each datapoint x in X and for each tree, return the index of the leaf x ends up
                   in. Leaves are numbered within [0; 2**(self.max_depth+1)), possibly with gaps in the                   numbering.



--- PAGE 212 ---

              Return type

                    array_like, shape=[n_samples, n_trees]
     property best_iteration:  int(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#int) 
            The best iteration obtained by early stopping. This attribute is 0-based, for instance if the best iteration is
             the first round, then best_iteration is 0.
     property best_score:  float(https://docs.python.org/3.10/library/functions.html#float) 
            The best score obtained by early stopping.
     property coef_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray)

 ò  Note
  Coefficients are defined only for linear learners
  Coefficients are only defined when the linear model is chosen as base learner (booster=gblinear ). It is
  not defined for other base learner types, such as tree learners (booster=gbtree  ).
```

             Returns

              Return type

     evals_result()

            Return the evaluation results.

            If eval_set is passed to the fit()(#page=212) function, you can call evals_result() to get evaluation results for all            passed eval_sets. When eval_metric is also passed to the fit()(#page=212) function, the evals_result will contain            the eval_metrics passed to the fit()(#page=212) function.
            The returned evaluation result is a dictionary:


```
 {'validation_0': {'logloss': ['0.604835', '0.531479']},
  'validation_1': {'logloss': ['0.41965', '0.17686']}}
```


              Return type

                     evals_result

     property feature_importances_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray) 
           Feature importances property, return depends on importance_type parameter. When model trained with
            multi-class/multi-label/multi-target dataset, the feature importance is “averaged” over all targets. The “average” is defined based on the importance type. For instance, if the importance type is “total_gain”, then             the score is sum of loss change for each split from all trees.

             Returns
                    • feature_importances_ (array of shape [n_features] except for multi-class)
                     • linear model, which returns an array with shape (n_features, n_classes)
     property feature_names_in_:  ndarray(https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray) 
           Names of features seen during fit()(#page=212). Defined only when X has feature names that are all strings.
